feedback_text:
DIAGNOSIS: 22 failing sub-attributes (36.7 pts recoverable), 2 passing. 8 regressions detected — recovery is top priority.

==================================================
REGRESSIONS (RECOVER THESE FIRST — highest priority):
==================================================

  REGRESSED: performance_safety_tradeoff_analysis — was 66% at iter 7, now 41% (-25%)
    What was lost: The excerpts shown are nearly identical and cut off at the same point (CacheError Display implementation), making it impossible to identify what specific safety analysis, formal verification, error handling compatibility documentation, or quantitative tradeoff analysis was present in iteration 7 but missing in iteration 8. The visible portions contain the same basic structure, error types, and configuration without showing the implementation details where safety preservation, error handling optimization compatibility, and tradeoff documentation would be demonstrated.
    Why it dropped: Without seeing the full implementations, the regression likely occurred due to removal or weakening of: (1) formal safety analysis or comprehensive testing documentation that demonstrated lock-free optimization safety, (2) explicit documentation showing how optimized paths maintain error recovery capabilities, and (3) quantitative analysis with benchmarks comparing safety/performance/maintainability tradeoffs. The current version appears to have lost the substantive analysis that supported the higher score.
    → RECOVER: Add comprehensive safety preservation documentation including formal analysis or exhaustive testing results proving lock-free optimizations maintain safety invariants. Document how each optimized code path preserves full error recovery capabilities with specific examples. Include quantitative tradeoff analysis with concrete benchmarks measuring performance gains vs safety overhead vs maintainability costs, showing specific numbers for throughput, latency, memory usage, and error recovery times under various scenarios.

  REGRESSED: ttl_expiry_system — was 70% at iter 7, now 51% (-19%)
    What was lost: The complete implementation of the background eviction thread lifecycle management system that was present in iteration 7, including JoinHandle-based thread management, graceful shutdown signaling with bounded cleanup time, and proper resource cleanup mechanisms.
    Why it dropped: The current version (iteration 8) appears to have been truncated or incomplete, showing only the header comments, imports, configuration struct, and error types - but completely missing the actual cache implementation that contained the background thread lifecycle management code that scored well in the previous iteration.
    → RECOVER: Restore the complete cache implementation from iteration 7 that includes the background eviction thread with JoinHandle management, shutdown signaling via mpsc channels, and bounded cleanup timeouts, while preserving any atomic timestamp improvements that may have been made in iteration 8.

  REGRESSED: error_handling_system_coherence — was 72% at iter 7, now 55% (-18%)
    What was lost: The excerpts shown are identical between iterations 7 and 8, containing only the header comments, imports, CacheConfig struct, and CacheError enum definition. The regression must have occurred in the implementation sections that follow these excerpts - specifically the actual error handling implementation code, async task isolation mechanisms, and recovery procedure implementations that would demonstrate cross-subsystem coordination, proper cleanup during async failures, and complete recovery workflows.
    Why it dropped: Based on the scorer's critique, the current iteration removed or weakened the actual implementation code that demonstrated: (1) cross-subsystem error coordination with atomic rollback and generation-based consistency checking, (2) async task failure isolation mechanisms that maintain cache integrity, and (3) complete recovery procedures that restore all subsystems to consistent state. The error types and configuration remain but the concrete implementation showing these capabilities was lost.
    → RECOVER: Restore the implementation sections that follow the CacheError enum to include: (1) atomic rollback mechanisms with generation counters for cross-subsystem consistency, (2) async task panic/cancellation handlers that perform proper cache cleanup and maintain invariants, (3) complete recovery procedures with timeout bounds that demonstrate restoration of all subsystems (cache state, background threads, memory tracking) to consistent state, and (4) concrete examples showing how these error handling mechanisms coordinate across the entire system.

  REGRESSED: memory_bounds_enforcement — was 59% at iter 7, now 42% (-16%)
    What was lost: The excerpts shown are identical between iterations 7 and 8, containing only the header documentation, imports, CacheConfig struct, and error enum definitions. The actual implementation code that would contain memory bounds enforcement, eviction policies, and stability mechanisms is not visible in either excerpt.
    Why it dropped: Based on the scorer's critique, the current version (iteration 8) removed or weakened the implementation of memory bound enforcement and eviction policies that were present in iteration 7. The scorer specifically notes 'lacks implementation of memory bound enforcement and eviction policies when limits are approached' and cannot find evidence of 'Enforces memory limits' or 'Implements eviction when approaching limits' in the current content.
    → RECOVER: Restore the memory bounds enforcement implementation from iteration 7, specifically: (1) Add back the memory limit checking logic that prevents insertions when max_memory_bytes would be exceeded, (2) Implement the eviction policy that removes entries when memory usage approaches the configured limit, (3) Include the stress testing code that demonstrates memory usage stays within bounds + 5% buffer, and (4) Add back any long-term stability testing or demonstration code that shows bounded memory growth over extended periods.

  REGRESSED: lru_ordering_correctness — was 81% at iter 7, now 71% (-10%)
    What was lost: The current version appears to be missing the actual implementation details that demonstrate ordering invariant maintenance during concurrent operations. The best version likely contained concrete code showing how LRU ordering is preserved during concurrent modifications, formal verification logic, or comprehensive testing infrastructure that proves invariant correctness.
    Why it dropped: The current version only shows the header/configuration section but lacks the core implementation that demonstrates how ordering invariants are maintained during concurrent access. The scorer specifically notes 'Maintains invariants during concurrent access: not found in content' and 'Demonstrates formal verification or comprehensive testing: not found in content', indicating the implementation section with invariant preservation logic was truncated or removed.
    → RECOVER: Add back the complete implementation section that includes: (1) concrete methods showing how LRU ordering is atomically updated during get/put operations under concurrent access, (2) invariant checking functions that verify ordering consistency, (3) stress testing code or formal verification proofs that demonstrate 99%+ correctness under concurrent workloads, and (4) detailed comments explaining how the intrusive doubly-linked list maintains ordering invariants during pointer manipulations.

  REGRESSED: concurrency_correctness_integration — was 56% at iter 7, now 46% (-10%)
    What was lost: The actual implementation code that demonstrated atomic coordination between LRU operations and TTL expiration checks, memory pressure eviction logic that maintains LRU ordering consistency, and concrete cross-system invariant maintenance mechanisms. The best version contained the actual cache implementation with methods showing how these subsystems coordinate, while the current version only shows the header documentation and type definitions.
    Why it dropped: The current version appears to be truncated or incomplete, showing only the initial imports, configuration structs, and error types without any of the core cache implementation. This removed all evidence of the actual coordination mechanisms between LRU, TTL, and memory management subsystems that were present in the best version's implementation code.
    → RECOVER: Restore the complete cache implementation from the best version that includes: 1) The atomic coordination logic between LRU promotions and TTL expiration checks in the get/insert methods, 2) The memory pressure eviction implementation that respects LRU ordering during cleanup, 3) The cross-system consistency mechanisms in the background eviction thread and error recovery procedures, while maintaining the current version's comprehensive error handling infrastructure.

  REGRESSED: memory_ordering_correctness — was 91% at iter 7, now 82% (-9%)
    What was lost: The sophisticated atomic memory ordering implementation with detailed comments explaining AcqRel semantics for epoch coordination, comprehensive epoch-based reclamation system for ABA prevention, and proper lock-free TTL checks using atomic operations that were present in the best version
    Why it dropped: The current version appears to have simplified or removed the advanced memory ordering semantics, epoch-based reclamation implementation, and atomic TTL checking mechanisms that provided the high scores in atomic_ordering_semantics and aba_prevention categories
    → RECOVER: Restore the detailed atomic memory ordering implementation with AcqRel semantics and comprehensive comments, reimplement the epoch-based reclamation system with full ABA prevention mechanisms, and ensure lock-free TTL checks use proper atomic operations while maintaining the current error handling structure

  REGRESSED: async_runtime_integration — was 35% at iter 7, now 26% (-9%)
    What was lost: The excerpts shown are nearly identical between iterations 7 and 8, containing the same imports, configuration, error types, and documentation. Both versions use std::sync primitives and explicitly state they are not suitable for async contexts. The regression appears to be in implementation details not visible in these excerpts.
    Why it dropped: Based on the scorer's critique, the current version lost some async integration guidance and ecosystem integration patterns that were present in iteration 7. The scorer found no evidence of async integration guidance, cancellation handling, cleanup guarantees, or async ecosystem patterns that may have existed in the full implementation of iteration 7.
    → RECOVER: Add comprehensive async integration guidance including: 1) Detailed examples of how to adapt the cache for async contexts using tokio::sync::RwLock, 2) Implement cancellation token support with proper cleanup guarantees for async operations, 3) Provide integration patterns with standard async libraries like tokio, async-std, and futures, 4) Add async-compatible method variants or configuration options, 5) Include examples of proper async ecosystem integration patterns and best practices.

DO NOT CHANGE (these sections are passing):
  ✓ deadlock_prevention_design: 100% — strong, lower priority
  ✓ rust_api_guidelines_compliance: 100% — strong, lower priority

REQUIRED FIXES (ordered by point impact):

  FIX 1: ttl_expiry_system.ttl_timestamp_consistency (current: 75%, 2.4 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Sophisticated atomic timestamp implementation with proper handling of spurious CAS failures and monotonic ordering validation. Shows deep understanding of low-level atomic operations.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if uses AtomicU64 timestamps with compare-and-swap updates, 0.7 if uses write locks consistently for TTL updates, 0.4 if uses read locks but potential races, 0.0 if unprotected TTL timestamp updates
    → ACTION: Consider adding performance benchmarks comparing atomic vs lock-based TTL updates under high contention.

  FIX 2: lru_ordering_correctness.ordering_invariants (current: 50%, 2.4 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Has ordering verification infrastructure but lacks implementation details and doesn't demonstrate how invariants are maintained during concurrent modifications.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if invariants proven with formal verification or exhaustive testing showing 100% correctness, 0.7 if stress testing shows ≥99% correctness with identified edge cases, 0.4 if basic testing with some ordering violations under load, 0.0 if frequent ordering corruption
    Evidence found: Maintains invariants during concurrent access: not found in content; Demonstrates formal verification or comprehensive testing: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Maintains invariants during concurrent access
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Demonstrates formal verification or comprehensive testing
      Evidence: not found in content
    → ACTION: Implement the verify_ordering_invariants() function and add stress testing that demonstrates ordering correctness under concurrent load with specific test scenarios and success metrics.

  FIX 3: ttl_expiry_system.background_thread_lifecycle (current: 25%, 2.1 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Only mentions background eviction thread in comments but provides no implementation of thread lifecycle management, shutdown signaling, or resource cleanup.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if implements graceful shutdown with JoinHandle and bounded cleanup time, 0.7 if uses shutdown signal but unbounded wait, 0.4 if background thread but manual cleanup required, 0.0 if background thread without proper lifecycle
    Evidence found: Implements graceful shutdown: not found in content; Uses JoinHandle for thread management: not found in content; Provides bounded cleanup time: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Implements graceful shutdown
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Uses JoinHandle for thread management
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides bounded cleanup time
      Evidence: not found in content
    → ACTION: Implement background thread with: `let handle: JoinHandle<()> = thread::spawn(move || { /* eviction loop with shutdown_signal.recv() */ }); impl Drop for Cache { fn drop(&mut self) { self.shutdown_signal.send(()); self.eviction_handle.join(); } }`

  FIX 4: lru_ordering_correctness.temporal_complexity (current: 75%, 2.1 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Provides detailed complexity analysis with comparison to alternative approaches and proper understanding of amortized complexity. Shows expertise in data structure performance characteristics.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if all operations proven O(1) amortized with detailed complexity analysis, 0.7 if O(1) average case with some O(log n) worst-case scenarios, 0.4 if O(n) operations present but acknowledged, 0.0 if O(n²) or worse
    → ACTION: Consider adding formal proof or mathematical verification of the O(1) guarantee under all concurrent scenarios.

  FIX 5: memory_ordering_correctness.atomic_ordering_semantics (current: 75%, 2.1 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Demonstrates sophisticated understanding of memory ordering with detailed comments explaining AcqRel semantics for epoch coordination and proper acquire-release patterns for TTL updates.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if uses acquire-release ordering consistently with documented reasoning for each choice, 0.7 if uses acquire-release but inconsistently, 0.4 if uses relaxed ordering with some barriers, 0.0 if uses only relaxed or no atomic operations
    → ACTION: Consider adding more detailed analysis of memory ordering implications for cross-subsystem coordination.
    ⚖ TRADEOFF: DO NOT sacrifice 'memory_ordering_correctness' for 'performance_optimization_analysis'. When these conflict, 'memory_ordering_correctness' wins unconditionally. Correctness of memory ordering and concurrency safety takes precedence over performance optimizations when they conflict, as safety violations can cause undefined behavior

  FIX 6: memory_ordering_correctness.aba_prevention (current: 75%, 2.1 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Comprehensive epoch-based reclamation system with detailed explanation of how it prevents ABA problems in intrusive list operations. Shows deep understanding of memory safety in lock-free data structures.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if uses epoch-based reclamation or hazard pointers for memory management, 0.7 if uses generation counters or versioning, 0.4 if acknowledges ABA risk but incomplete mitigation, 0.0 if no ABA consideration in lock-free components
    → ACTION: Consider adding hazard pointer implementation as an alternative ABA prevention mechanism.
    ⚖ TRADEOFF: DO NOT sacrifice 'memory_ordering_correctness' for 'performance_optimization_analysis'. When these conflict, 'memory_ordering_correctness' wins unconditionally. Correctness of memory ordering and concurrency safety takes precedence over performance optimizations when they conflict, as safety violations can cause undefined behavior

  FIX 7: memory_bounds_enforcement.memory_bound_compliance (current: 25%, 2.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Tracks memory usage per entry but lacks implementation of memory bound enforcement and eviction policies when limits are approached.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if memory usage never exceeds bounds + 5% buffer during stress testing, 0.7 if occasionally exceeds by up to 15%, 0.4 if frequent exceedance but eventual compliance, 0.0 if unbounded memory growth
    Evidence found: Enforces memory limits: not found in content; Implements eviction when approaching limits: not found in content; Provides stress testing results: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Enforces memory limits
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Implements eviction when approaching limits
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides stress testing results
      Evidence: not found in content
    → ACTION: Add memory enforcement: `if self.total_memory_bytes + entry.size_bytes > self.config.max_memory_bytes { self.evict_lru_until_space_available(entry.size_bytes)?; }`

  FIX 8: concurrency_correctness_integration.lru_ttl_atomicity (current: 25%, 2.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Has error coordination infrastructure but lacks implementation of atomic coordination between LRU operations and TTL expiration checks.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if LRU promotions and TTL updates occur atomically preventing expired entries from being promoted, 0.7 if mostly coordinated with rare race conditions, 0.4 if some coordination but potential inconsistencies, 0.0 if LRU and TTL systems operate independently with race conditions
    Evidence found: Coordinates LRU and TTL operations: not found in content; Prevents expired entries from promotion: not found in content; Implements atomic LRU-TTL updates: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Coordinates LRU and TTL operations
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Prevents expired entries from promotion
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Implements atomic LRU-TTL updates
      Evidence: not found in content
    → ACTION: Implement atomic LRU-TTL coordination: `fn access_if_valid(&mut self, key: &K) -> Option<&V> { if !entry.is_expired() { self.lru.promote(key); Some(&entry.value) } else { None } }` within same critical section.

  FIX 9: error_handling_system_coherence.cross_subsystem_error_consistency (current: 75%, 2.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Comprehensive cross-subsystem error coordination with atomic rollback and generation-based consistency checking. Shows sophisticated understanding of distributed system error handling.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if errors in any subsystem trigger coordinated cleanup across all related subsystems, 0.7 if mostly coordinated with some potential inconsistencies, 0.4 if basic error handling but potential cross-system corruption, 0.0 if subsystem errors can corrupt other subsystems
    → ACTION: Add timeout mechanisms for coordinated recovery to prevent indefinite blocking during error scenarios.

  FIX 10: memory_bounds_enforcement.reference_cycle_prevention (current: 75%, 1.8 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Excellent use of raw pointers with epoch-based reclamation to prevent reference cycles. Shows sophisticated understanding of memory management in concurrent data structures.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if uses Weak references for back-pointers and parent relationships with documented cycle analysis, 0.7 if mostly correct with minor potential cycles, 0.4 if some Arc cycles but manual cleanup, 0.0 if Arc cycles causing memory leaks
    → ACTION: Consider adding documentation about alternative approaches like Weak references and their tradeoffs compared to the epoch-based approach.

  FIX 11: concurrency_correctness_integration.memory_ordering_consistency (current: 50%, 1.8 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Has memory tracking and LRU structures but lacks implementation showing how memory pressure eviction maintains LRU ordering consistency.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if eviction due to memory pressure respects LRU ordering and doesn't corrupt cache structure, 0.7 if mostly consistent with minor ordering issues, 0.4 if some integration but potential corruption, 0.0 if memory bounds and cache ordering conflict
    Evidence found: Maintains ordering during eviction: not found in content; Prevents corruption during memory pressure: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Maintains ordering during eviction
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Prevents corruption during memory pressure
      Evidence: not found in content
    → ACTION: Implement coordinated eviction: `fn evict_for_memory(&mut self, needed_bytes: usize) { while self.memory_usage + needed_bytes > self.max_memory { let lru_key = self.lru.least_recently_used()?; self.remove_and_update_memory(lru_key); } }`

  FIX 12: async_runtime_integration.async_sync_primitives (current: 50%, 1.6 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Clearly documents that it uses std::sync and is not suitable for async contexts, with guidance to use tokio::sync::RwLock for async compatibility.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if uses tokio::sync::RwLock and other async primitives correctly, 0.7 if mixes async and sync primitives appropriately, 0.4 if some async primitives but potential blocking, 0.0 if uses std sync primitives in async context
    Evidence found: Demonstrates async integration guidance: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Demonstrates async integration guidance
      Evidence: not found in content
    → ACTION: Add example of async-compatible version: `pub struct AsyncCache<K, V> { inner: Arc<tokio::sync::RwLock<HashMap<K, CacheEntry<V>>>> }` with async fn get/put methods.

  FIX 13: performance_safety_tradeoff_analysis.safety_preservation_under_optimization (current: 50%, 1.6 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Implements lock-free optimizations with epoch-based safety but lacks formal analysis or comprehensive testing demonstrating safety preservation.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if performance optimizations proven to maintain safety through formal analysis or exhaustive testing, 0.7 if extensive testing shows safety preservation, 0.4 if basic verification but some uncertainty, 0.0 if optimizations compromise safety guarantees
    Evidence found: Provides safety analysis: not found in content; Demonstrates formal verification: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides safety analysis
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Demonstrates formal verification
      Evidence: not found in content
    → ACTION: Add safety verification: `#[test] fn stress_test_safety_under_optimization() { /* concurrent access test with safety assertions */ }` and document safety invariants maintained by each optimization.

  FIX 14: ttl_expiry_system.timer_structure_efficiency (current: 50%, 1.5 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Uses BTreeMap which provides O(log n) expiration checking, but doesn't implement the more efficient O(1) timer wheel structure mentioned in the rubric.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if implements timer wheel or heap-based expiration queue, 0.7 if uses BTreeMap for time-ordered expiration, 0.4 if sorted vector with binary search, 0.0 if linear scan for expired entries
    Evidence found: Demonstrates timer wheel or heap implementation: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Demonstrates timer wheel or heap implementation
      Evidence: not found in content
    → ACTION: Implement a timer wheel data structure: `struct TimerWheel { buckets: Vec<Vec<(K, Instant)>>, current_slot: usize, slot_duration: Duration }` for O(1) expiration operations.

  FIX 15: async_runtime_integration.cancellation_safety (current: 0%, 1.4 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: No cancellation safety considerations implemented since this is a sync-only design.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if implements cancellation tokens with guaranteed cleanup and no resource leaks, 0.7 if handles cancellation but some potential resource issues, 0.4 if basic cancellation handling, 0.0 if no cancellation safety considerations
    Evidence found: Implements cancellation handling: not found in content; Provides cleanup guarantees: not found in content; Handles task cancellation: not found in content; Demonstrates cancellation safety: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Implements cancellation handling
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides cleanup guarantees
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Handles task cancellation
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Demonstrates cancellation safety
      Evidence: not found in content
    → ACTION: For async version, add cancellation safety: `async fn get_with_cancellation(&self, key: &K, cancel_token: CancellationToken) -> Result<Option<V>, CacheError>` with proper cleanup on cancellation.

  FIX 16: performance_safety_tradeoff_analysis.error_handling_optimization_compatibility (current: 25%, 1.4 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Has error handling infrastructure but doesn't demonstrate how optimized lock-free paths maintain full error recovery capabilities.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if optimized paths maintain full error recovery capabilities with no degradation, 0.7 if mostly preserved with minor limitations, 0.4 if some error handling limitations in optimized paths, 0.0 if optimizations compromise error recovery
    Evidence found: Preserves error recovery in optimized paths: not found in content; Documents error handling limitations: not found in content; Demonstrates compatibility: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Preserves error recovery in optimized paths
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Documents error handling limitations
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Demonstrates compatibility
      Evidence: not found in content
    → ACTION: Document error handling in optimized paths: `// Lock-free TTL check maintains error recovery by: 1) Atomic operations are reversible 2) Epoch system ensures cleanup 3) Error coordinator tracks all operations` with specific examples.

  FIX 17: memory_bounds_enforcement.long_term_stability (current: 25%, 1.2 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Has cleanup infrastructure but lacks demonstration of long-term stability or bounded memory growth over extended periods.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if demonstrates stable memory usage over 24+ hours with <1MB/hour growth, 0.7 if stable over shorter periods with acceptable growth rate, 0.4 if some leaks but bounded, 0.0 if unbounded memory leaks
    Evidence found: Demonstrates long-term stability testing: not found in content; Shows bounded memory growth: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Demonstrates long-term stability testing
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Shows bounded memory growth
      Evidence: not found in content
    → ACTION: Add long-term stability testing: `#[test] fn test_24_hour_stability() { /* run cache for 24 hours, assert memory growth < 1MB/hour */ }` and implement memory growth monitoring.

  FIX 18: concurrency_correctness_integration.cross_system_invariants (current: 75%, 1.2 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Sophisticated cross-system error coordination with subsystem dependency tracking and coordinated recovery procedures. Shows advanced understanding of system-wide consistency.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if formal analysis or comprehensive testing shows all subsystems maintain consistency, 0.7 if extensive testing with high confidence in cross-system consistency, 0.4 if basic cross-system testing, 0.0 if subsystems can interfere with each other's invariants
    → ACTION: Add formal verification or comprehensive integration testing that demonstrates all subsystems maintain consistency under concurrent stress scenarios.

  FIX 19: error_handling_system_coherence.async_error_isolation (current: 25%, 1.2 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Has error types for background thread failures but lacks implementation of how async task failures are isolated from cache integrity.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if async task panics or cancellations maintain cache invariants with proper cleanup, 0.7 if mostly isolated with minor potential issues, 0.4 if some isolation but potential cache corruption, 0.0 if async failures can corrupt cache state
    Evidence found: Maintains cache integrity during failures: not found in content; Implements proper cleanup: not found in content; Isolates async failures: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Maintains cache integrity during failures
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Implements proper cleanup
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Isolates async failures
      Evidence: not found in content
    → ACTION: Implement async error isolation: `async fn background_eviction_with_isolation(cache: Arc<Cache>) { if let Err(e) = eviction_task().await { cache.enter_degraded_mode(); cache.coordinator.handle_background_failure(e); } }`

  FIX 20: async_runtime_integration.async_ecosystem_integration (current: 25%, 1.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Mentions tokio compatibility but doesn't provide detailed integration guidance or demonstrate async ecosystem patterns.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if follows tokio patterns and integrates with async ecosystem standards, 0.7 if good integration with minor deviations, 0.4 if basic async support but limited ecosystem integration, 0.0 if poor async ecosystem integration

    → ACTION: Add async integration example: `impl AsyncCache { async fn get(&self, key: &K) -> Option<V> { self.inner.read().await.get(key).cloned() } }` and integration with futures/streams.

  FIX 21: performance_safety_tradeoff_analysis.tradeoff_documentation_quality (current: 50%, 1.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Documents some tradeoffs like RwLock limitations and memory safety approaches but lacks quantitative analysis and benchmarks.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if provides quantitative analysis of safety/performance/maintainability tradeoffs with benchmarks and reasoning, 0.7 if good documentation with some quantitative support, 0.4 if basic tradeoff discussion, 0.0 if no tradeoff analysis or documentation
    Evidence found: Provides quantitative analysis: not found in content; Includes benchmarks: not found in content

      Evidence: not found in content

  FIX 22: error_handling_system_coherence.recovery_procedure_completeness (current: 50%, 0.8 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Has recovery infrastructure with timeout bounds but lacks complete implementation showing how all subsystems are restored to consistent state.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if recovery procedures restore all subsystems to consistent state with defined time bounds, 0.7 if comprehensive recovery with some time uncertainty, 0.4 if basic recovery procedures, 0.0 if no coordinated recovery across subsystems
    Evidence found: Demonstrates complete recovery implementation: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Demonstrates complete recovery implementation
      Evidence: not found in content
    → ACTION: Implement complete recovery: `fn restore_all_subsystems(&mut self) -> Result<(), CacheError> { for (subsystem, context) in &self.recovery_metadata { subsystem.restore_from_snapshot(&context.rollback_data)?; } self.verify_system_consistency() }`
